serving.natural_language_portion: One medium golden kiwi, peeled, weighs approximately 90 g and provides around 57 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Golden Kiwifruit, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values retained as-is. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and select micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 168153, Kiwifruit, gold, raw) where HPB does not report them.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Kiwifruit, golden variety
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 58 (medium) for golden kiwifruit (Actinidia chinensis) is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) AJCN 76(1):5–56. Glycemic load per 90 g serving = (58 × 15.79 × 0.90) / 100 = 8.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (12.3 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.4 g/100 g) triggers the solid-food sugar gate despite very low sodium (3 mg) and high potassium (315 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Limit portion to half a fruit (~50 g) to reduce the insulin-mediated sodium-retention effect of the high sugar load, and pair with a protein or fibre source; the high potassium content is otherwise beneficial for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (58) combined with high sugar (12.3 g/100 g) and low fibre (1.4 g/100 g) produces a moderate glycaemic load; fibre is insufficient to adequately blunt postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit golden kiwi to one small fruit (~70 g) per serving, prefer green kiwi (higher fibre, lower sugar), and pair with protein to reduce glycaemic impact; monitor postprandial glucose response individually.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g), zero trans fat, high vitamin C (161 mg/100 g), high potassium (315 mg/100 g), and vitamin E (1.46 mg/100 g) support cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Golden kiwi is a heart-friendly fruit; its high vitamin C and potassium content support endothelial function and blood pressure regulation, and its negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol make it suitable for a cardioprotective diet in moderate portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Exceptionally high vitamin C (~161 mg/100 g) reduces oxidative stress in RA-affected joints; nightshade-free with negligible saturated fat.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Golden kiwi is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its high antioxidant vitamin C content; consume in moderation as excess sugar may contribute to systemic inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Golden kiwi (Actinidia chinensis), also known as golden kiwifruit or yellow kiwi, is a fresh tropical fruit distinguished from the more common green kiwi by its smooth, hairless brown skin, bright yellow flesh, and sweeter, less tart flavour profile with tropical notes reminiscent of mango and citrus. It is consumed raw, typically peeled and sliced or eaten by halving and scooping. Per 100 g, golden kiwi provides 63 kcal, 15.79 g carbohydrate, 12.3 g sugar, 1.4 g dietary fibre, 1.02 g protein, and only 0.28 g fat. It is an outstanding source of vitamin C (~161 mg/100 g, exceeding 100% of daily requirements), and also provides potassium (315 mg), calcium (17 mg), and vitamin E (1.46 mg). Its medium glycemic index (GI 58) and relatively high sugar content with low fibre mean it should be consumed in moderation by individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Clinically, its high vitamin C and polyphenol content make it beneficial for immune function, skin health, and reducing oxidative stress in inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. It is commercially grown primarily in New Zealand and Italy and is widely available in Singapore supermarkets year-round.
common_user_queries: Is golden kiwi good for diabetics?
Difference between golden kiwi and green kiwi
Is golden kiwi high in sugar?
Can I eat golden kiwi if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95. Micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 168153) for golden kiwifruit, an exact match. GI value from University of Sydney GI Database with peer-reviewed corroboration. All values are internally consistent.
notes: Saturated fat (0.02 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.03 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.17 g), trans_fat_g (0.0 g), and micronutrients (vitamin C, A, B-vitamins, D, E, minerals, water) sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168153 (Kiwifruit, gold, raw) to supplement HPB data which does not report these fields. Trans fat is 0.0 g: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for raw golden kiwifruit. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg and vitamin D is 0.0 mcg: naturally zero as this is a plant-based fruit containing no animal-derived nutrients. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg: naturally zero as this is a plant food. The input health_suitability condition key 'diabetes' has been mapped to the required 'type2_diabetes' key with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. GI of 58 for golden kiwi is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database; green kiwi has a lower GI (~52) due to higher fibre content. Latex-fruit syndrome cross-reactivity noted as a potential allergen concern for susceptible individuals.
